data.title: H&M Earnings Beat Market Expectations as Turnaround Efforts Continue — Update
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

STOCKHOLM--Hennes & Mauritz reported a quarterly earnings beat, sending shares higher as investors cheered its turnaround efforts, while well-received collections got fall performance off to a good start.

Shares in the company rose as much as 11% in early European trade.

The Swedish fast-fashion retailer is working through a restructuring to boost sales that includes investing in new product ranges and upgrading its digital channels and stores, while trying to attract new customers through a marketing push.

It has also started to offer collections at broader price points and is working to bring more manufacturing closer to its major markets to respond to changing trends and aid quicker and smoother logistics.

Chief Executive Daniel Erver said in a statement that earnings strengthened in the quarter thanks to a stronger customer offering, improved gross margin and good cost control.

Fall collections are proving popular and H&M said sales in September--the first month of the company's fourth quarter--are expected to be on par with the same month last year in local currencies. The company reported 11% sales growth in September last year.

An uncertain economic environment and concerns related to U.S. President Trump's tariffs have created challenges though, just as the company faces increasing competition from low-cost online platforms such as Shein and Temu as well as its slightly higher-end rival, Zara-owner Inditex.

"While we continue to drive our plan forward, the world around us remains uncertain," Erver said on an analyst call Thursday.

"With geopolitical challenges and a cautious consumer, it becomes even more important for us to stay focused on what we can influence and where we can make the biggest difference."

Despite working to bring more production closer to its main markets, H&M still counts Asia as a large sourcing region, leaving it vulnerable to transportation delays, not least because of disruption in the Red Sea. To counter the issue it has been holding more stock on hand to ensure it has plentiful supply.

Inventory levels fell 9% in the third quarter and with a more efficient and flexible supply chain and more purchasing in season, there are opportunities to improve the inventory position again in the fourth quarter, it said.

Discounting will rise somewhat in the fourth quarter, but that is partly explained by Black Friday falling one day earlier than last year, it added.

External factors, such as exchange rates and shipping costs, should have a positive effect in the fourth quarter, though their boost to gross margin will be held back by higher tariff costs.

Sales in its fiscal third quarter--which runs from June through August--rose 2% in local currencies, in line with a UBS forecast, despite having 4% fewer stores at the end of the quarter compared with last year.

The company reported a gross margin of 52.9% in the quarter, versus the 51.3% analysts at UBS had expected. The operating margin rose to 8.6% from 5.9% a year earlier.

H&M reported an operating profit of 4.91 billion Swedish kronor ($522.2 million) for the third quarter to Aug. 31, compared with 3.51 billion kronor a year earlier as sales fell 3.4% to 57.02 billion kronor. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected operating profit of 3.7 billion kronor on sales of 57.03 billion kronor.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com

data.title: Hungarian forint under pressure as investors take profits
data.description:
The Hungarian forint weakened on Thursday as investors took profits after its recent strengthening, while the Czech crown and Polish zloty were little changed.

On Tuesday, Hungary's central bank continued a year-long pause in rate easing and said it must maintain tight policy to curb inflation. Hungary's key rate is the joint-highest in the European Union at 6.50%.

The forint was 0.2% weaker on Thursday at 0756 GMT, trading at 391.9000 to the euro.

"As the dollar is strengthening, there is some profit-taking ... also, we have been expecting a correction after weeks of continuous strengthening in the forint," an FX trader in Budapest said.

"But I do not think we will go back as far as 400, especially as the central bank was still pretty strong in its communication on Tuesday. Also, there is not a lot of liquidity."

The Hungarian central bank's hawkish tone has helped boost the currency thanks to the high rate differential to the euro zone, just as the Czech central bank's hawkishness helped the crown.

The Czech National Bank kept interest rates unchanged for the third time in a row on Wednesday and said tight policy was still needed in view of inflation risks coming from the services sector, a strong property market and fast wage growth.

The crown was stable at 24.3050 per euro.

"Yesterday’s CNB board meeting was as expected and brought no surprises ... CNB’s hawkish rhetoric will continue to support the exchange rate," Commerzbank wrote in a note to clients.

In Poland, where the central bank has started cutting rates after a long pause, the zloty was up 0.1% at 4.2635.

Polish central bank Governor Adam Glapinski signalled caution on further decisions after a cut this month, as loose fiscal policy, rapid economic growth and wage pressures boosted inflation risks.

"The EUR/PLN exchange rate remains at yesterday's closing level of 4.2670, and from a technical perspective, a move towards 4.28, the upper limit of the zloty's consolidation channel, is possible, especially if the euro/dollar declines," Bank Millennium analysts wrote.

"However, after the EUR/USD exchange rate reaches around 1.1750, an important technical level, a slight upward rebound is possible ... The (zloty) valuation will remain under the dominant influence of global sentiment and technical conditions."

The zloty has mostly shaken off negative outlooks given by two credit ratings agencies this month over concerns about budget deficits.

Stock markets in the region were mixed, with Budapest stable, Warsaw losing 0.6%, Prague rising 0.2% and Bucharest gaining 0.84%.

data.title: Beneteau drops after H1 miss, Portzamparc cuts PT on 'poor' guidance
data.description:
** Shares in Beneteau drop around 7% after the French boat builder's H1 earnings missed expectations, with Portzamparc pointing to weak H2 guidance

** The broker says H1 earnings were weaker than expected, hit by lower activity, margin normalisation, and extra costs

** The H2 guidance was a "bad surprise", Portzamparc says, adding that margins face pressure from promotions, an unfavourable EUR/USD rate, US tariffs, and costly ramp-ups at new sites

** Broker believes 2025 could land below current consensus

** It cuts its PT on the stock to EUR 9.50 from EUR 11.00 on "poor" second-half guidance, but keeps its "accumulate" rating

data.title: Asian FX pressured by firm dollar, fiscal concerns undermine Indonesian rupiah
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indonesian rupiah down for sixth session
  • Thai baht falls to three-week low
  • Philippine peso hits lowest level since August 1

By Himanshi Akhand

The Indonesian rupiah was pinned near its weakest level in five months on Thursday as emerging Asian currencies fell to multi-month lows weighed down by a firm U.S. dollar.

The rupiah fell for a sixth consecutive session, its longest losing streak since mid-December. It was last down 0.4% at its lowest point since late April.

Bank Indonesia's shock interest rate cut last week was viewed as bowing to President Prabowo Subianto's push to fast-track growth, which could jeopardize the country's fiscal credibility.

This has piled pressure on the rupiah, which was already plagued by unrest in the country and the abrupt removal of a long-serving finance minister.

"Recent policy push towards supporting growth via expansionary fiscal policy and dovish monetary policy combination can be seen as marginally negative for IDR," analysts at BofA said in a note.

"Liquidity injections and rate cuts would offset the impact of further Fed rate cuts by keeping rate differentials steady and reducing the carry appeal for IDR."

BofA analysts see the rupiah trading around 16,300 per dollar by the end of this year, assuming marginal appreciation in the fourth quarter due to a weaker greenback.

Thailand's baht fell 0.2% to a three-week low. It broke above the 32-per-dollar mark in the previous session after holding below it for three weeks. Authorities are acting to rein in the currency's gain, which is hurting exports and tourism.

Ratings agency Fitch revised its outlook for Thailand to "negative" from "stable", citing rising risks to public finance and lingering political uncertainty.

Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul said he would look to build confidence in the Thai economy. Meanwhile, the finance minister said the country needs to take heed of Fitch's warnings, adding that the cabinet would consider new stimulus measures next month.

Other regional currencies were also on the back foot: the Philippine peso slipped 0.8% to an eight-week low, while the Malaysian ringgit and Taiwan's dollar were at their weakest levels in at least two weeks.

An MSCI gauge of EM currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) was also around its lowest point in two weeks, as the dollar index held steady, with traders weighing the prospects of a measured Fed easing cycle.

Most regional stock markets were lower, with equities in Manila slipping 1.3% to their lowest level since mid-April.

Jakarta stocks slipped 0.5%, after touching a lifetime high in the previous session. Taiwan TWSE:TAIEX and Singapore stocks lost 0.7% and 0.3%, respectively.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** U.S. to seal Southeast Asia trade deals in coming months

** Korea's wobbles over US trade talks awaken the won bears

** Argentina assets rally as Bessent details strong US support

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0730 GMT - STV Group's absence of interim dividend isn't surprising, Panmure Liberum analysts Johnathan Barrett and Sean Kealy write in a note. The Scottish broadcaster didn't declare an interim dividend for the first-half due to the challenging environment against last year's interim dividend of 3.9 pence a share. The recent slowdown has placed significant pressure on its two divisions, they say. A market recovery would support improved profitability and potentially allow for the restart of dividends, they add. Shares are down 2.2% at 112 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0546 GMT - Kuaishou Technology's artificial-intelligence video generator Kling AI and other AI enablers could drive a re-rating in the Chinese tech company's shares, says DBS Group Research analysts in a note. They put Kling AI's addressable market at around US$60 billion in a bull-case scenario, citing the platform's improving model consistency. Kuaishou's generative AI-powered OneRec model could also offer upside in advertising and e-commerce through better recommendations. They project Kuaishou's 2025, 2026 and 2027 adjusted earnings to grow by 16%, 20% and 18%, respectively. DBS reinstates coverage on Kuaishou with a buy rating and a HK$98.00 target, citing its loyal and undermonetized user base. Shares gain 1.3% to HK$77.85. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0357 GMT - HD Hyundai Electric is well-positioned to improve its profit margins, partly supported by brisk gas-insulated-switchgear demand in the U.S., Nomura analyst Siwoo Kim writes in a note. Over 60% of the South Korean electrical-equipment maker's order backlog comes from North America on stronger-than-expected U.S. demand, Kim says. He points to the incremental GIS demand driven by constructions of artificial-intelligence data centers there. Revenue contribution from North America is expected to increase steadily from 35% currently over the coming quarters, he says. Nomura raises its target price for the company by 24% to KRW630,000 and keeps a neutral rating. Shares are 0.3% higher at KRW590,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0354 GMT - Alibaba Group's revised capital-expenditure guidance signals that demand for artificial-intelligence infrastructure is even stronger than expected, said Morningstar's Chelsey Tam. The Hangzhou-based company says it plans to increase spending on AI and cloud infrastructure beyond the 380 billion yuan pledged in February, and released a new large language model at its annual cloud conference in Hangzhou. "More demand means a more positive outlook," Tam said, underlining AI and cloud as bright spots for Alibaba. Markets seem to be upbeat on Alibaba's outlook for AI demand in China, Nomura's Jialong Shi adds. Shares of Alibaba rise 1.0% to HK$175.80. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0254 GMT - A multiyear semiconductor-industry boom is approaching, say Nomura analysts led by C.W. Chung in a research note. Big U.S. tech companies' investments in both artificial-intelligence and conventional servers are set to boost memory-chip demand and bring profit margins for DRAM and NAND products to record highs in 2026, they say. "Therefore, we believe the memory market is likely to experience an unprecedented super-cycle, with substantial investment continuing into 2027 and 2028," the analysts note. Nomura raises its target prices for the top memory-chip makers, Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, by 46% and 50%, respectively, reflecting an upgraded operating-profit outlook for both stocks. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0230 GMT - Tuas's underlying Ebitda margin could have been as high as 46% over its past fiscal year, according to its bull at Citi. Analyst William Park reckons that the Singapore-focused telecommunications provider's fiscal 2025 earnings included S$1.4 million of potential one-off costs, which covered asset disposal and most likely its ongoing acquisition of M1. Stripping out these costs, he thinks that the underlying Ebitda margin was as high as 46%, compared with the 45% reported. He reckons that the Australia-listed company could continue to beat expectations on costs. Citi keeps a buy rating and A$9.95 target price on the stock. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0131 GMT - SK Hynix is poised to get an earnings boost from its swift transition to high-bandwidth-memory 4 products, a more advanced artificial-intelligence chip component, Hanwha Investment & Securities analyst Kim Kwang-jin writes in a note. Kim points to the South Korean chip maker's completed HBM4 development recently, as its rivals are still working on customer samples. He expects SK Hynix to start supplying HBM4 products to clients on a small scale from end-2025 and on a large scale from 1Q 2026, ahead of its competitors. With SK Hynix set to take a significant HBM4 market share, he raises his 2026 operating-profit forecast for the company by 18% to KRW50.1 trillion. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1657 GMT - Lithium has quickly become a strategic resource essential to U.S. national security, clean energy, and economic competitiveness. The metal is used in a wide range of products from EVs, cell phones, and laptops to energy storage and medicine. While countries like Australia and Chile produce plenty of raw lithium, China dominates the processing, creating a supply chain vulnerability. For this reason, the U.S. is ramping up its involvement in lithium, from funding and regulation to potential ownership, because it sees the mineral as critical to its clean energy future and geopolitical independence. Jefferies analysts see a potential positive ripple effect if the U.S. government buys a stake in Lithium Americas, because investors are likely to see equity stakes as an indictor of a favorable return on invested capital. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1642 GMT - Micron Technology's outlook for gross margins was higher than most investors were expecting, analysts say. Some investors were looking for higher revenue numbers, Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoine Legault say, but margins guidance of 51.5% outshone even the most optimistic investors: "We don't think anyone was expecting MU to forecast >50% GMs," they say. This would be the first time gross margins surpass 50% since 2019, J.P. Morgan analysts say. Management said margins should continue to expand into the fiscal second quarter. Margins are looking even better than anticipated because tight supply is allowing Micron to raise prices. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1634 GMT - Micron Technology can keep up with funding its ambitious capital expenditure plans thanks to strong demand and limited supply of memory products, UBS analysts say. The company is continuing to ramp up capital expenditures into 2026, which has historically created problems for memory companies in the past. But the analysts believe Micron is in a better position because it is sitting on low inventory levels and customer inventory is healthy. "This capex is chasing a compute/memory demand pool that seems to be growing by leaps and bounds based on recent announcements by NVDA, OpenAI, Oracle, etc.," the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1414 GMT - Anthropic will drive significant revenue growth for Amazon Web Services, say Wells Fargo analysts, who upgrade Amazon to overweight from equal weight. The analysts increase their AWS revenue estimates by 3% for 2026 and 7% for 2027, largely due to higher expectations for revenue from Amazon and Anthropic's Project Rainier compute build-out. Meanwhile, market share losses for AWS will likely improve in 2026 and beyond as AWS accelerates, alleviating competitive concerns, the analysts say. Amazon is up 0.7% in early trading. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1220 GMT - French telecommunications companies might consider market consolidation, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. France remains among Europe's most competitive telecom markets, they say. However, the country's major players could pursue mergers or acquisitions, but for different reasons. Altice might be motivated by potential synergies and the opportunity to reverse previous debt write-downs. Bouygues could seek consolidation to achieve greater scale. Meanwhile, Iliad may aim to acquire assets to strengthen its service offering, and Orange would benefit from reduced competition to support growth. Still, a successful deal would require several factors to align simultaneously--particularly on pricing and asset distribution--making any transaction risky, they say. Orange shares are down 0.7%, and Bouygues shares are up 0.05%. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0613 GMT - Despite solid gains for MSCI's China Index since U.S. Liberation Day tariffs, it could rise further as the country's AI development gains momentum, Capital Economics says. "China is particularly well-positioned to benefit if enthusiasm for AI continues," notes Chief Markets Economist John Higgins. He notes the big-tech sector's weighting at around 60% of the MSCI China Index, higher than in its U.S. index. Noting that MSCI China's underperformance against the MSCI USA Index in recent years was largely due to faster U.S. EPS growth, Higgins says this EPS gap could narrow if China allows firms to benefit from plans to integrate AI across the economy. "And even if that didn't happen, the relative valuation of China's stock market remains lower than before the pandemic," he says. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0130 GMT - Forsyth Barr is happy with general insurer Tower's reset to its reinsurance costs. Analyst James Lindsay says Tower's renewal of its reinsurance program for FY 2026 was achieved on better-than-expected terms. Reinsurance costs are likely to fall from 13.3% of gross written premium in FY 2025 to 10.7% in FY 2026, Forsyth Barr says. Meanwhile, the maximum cover for the first two events has increased to NZ$915 million from NZ$800 million. "The program simplifies protection for large individual property risks, with Tower opting to retain increased underwriting risk in return for higher premiums," Forsyth Barr says. "We see this as a sensible trade-off in light of Tower's risk-based pricing capability, and view the placement of the program positively overall." It keeps an outperform call on Tower. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

0124 GMT - Srisawad Corp. looks on track for stronger earnings growth due to some tailwinds such as loan growth and net-interest-margin expansion, Thanachart Securities' Rawisara Suwanumphai says in a report. The financial services provider's loans could grow 3% in 2025, 8% in 2026 and 8% in 2027, partly driven by title loans, the analyst estimates. The Thai company's net interest margin should expand to 15.2% in 2026 and 15.4% in 2027 from 14.7% in 2025 thanks to growth in higher-yield title loans and declining funding costs. The brokerage forecasts Srisawad's earnings to grow 2% in 2H, 14% in 2026 and 13% in 2027. It raises its target price on the stock to THB36.00 from THB25.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at THB30.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2300 GMT - The sale of Deterra Royalties' gold offtake contracts returns its focus to industrial metals, differentiating the company from gold-royalty peers, Macquarie analysts say in a note. "Those looking to have exposure to the industrial metals complex for lower beta may find DRR an attractive alternative to higher operationally leveraged exposures," say the analysts. They reiterate a "neutral" rating and A$4.20 target. Deterra last traded at A$4.19. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2227 GMT [Dow Jones]--Regal Partners's share price continues to trade well below all-time highs, despite positive operating metrics that include an 8% rise in funds under management over July-August. Bell Potter believes this may relate to the conversion of various classes of stock into ordinary shares. "There are currently 356 million ordinary shares on issue, with 13 million shares due to convert at end-September, and a further 62 million shares converting in the next few years," says analyst Marcus Barnard. Bell Potter raises its price target by 15% to A$4.10/share, and says this assumes full conversion of the outstanding stock. Regal Partners ended Wednesday at A$2.92. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1215 GMT - The market seems too optimistic on Commerzbank's stock, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. Germany's fiscal stimulus plans have been driving investor enthusiasm and pushing shares higher, analysts write. Some of this is warranted given that earnings are expected to increase. "However, incorporating company targets...and these incremental gains, the stock currently trades at a 12% premium to the wider sector," they say, adding that they don't believe this is warranted. Consensus forecasts also appear optimistic and there is a risk that the M&A premium triggered by UniCredit's approach unravels over the next 12 months, they say. KBW downgrades the stock to underperform from market perform. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1126 GMT - KBC Group is more likely to focus on M&A plans in line with its strategy, JPMorgan says in a research note. This would entail domestic deals or those in the CEE region, JPM writes after Bloomberg reported that the Belgian lender was in the early stages of evaluating a potential deal for Dutch peer ABN Amro. "While never say never, strategically, we would assume that a potential transaction from KBC would likely be more focused on the domestic Belgian insurance market or the CEE region," the analysts write. KBC is interested in establishing a bancassurance model in the CEE region. KBC has set its sights on Belgian insurer Ethias, expressed interest in entering the Romanian market, and recently bought a bank in Slovakia. Capital deployment is one of the key debates around the stock, and management tends to announce a decision around full-year results, the analysts add.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1122 GMT - The rise in ABN Amro's shares earlier this week on the back of a Bloomberg report that suggested that KBC Group was in the early stages of considering a takeover is a reminder that the Dutch bank can be an attractive target, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. "An offer for ABN might have been financially attractive for KBC, but it would have come as a surprise," analyst Anke Reingen writes. Potential expression of interest is far from an actual transaction, as recent examples in the European banking sector have shown. BNP Paribas and Deutsche Bank are seen as potential suitors given their products' capabilities, she adds. "We do not think both feel the pressure to act, especially now, but might react if they see an opportunity disappear." (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1055 GMT - KBC Group has clear strategic M&A goals so the idea that it would seek a takeover of Dutch peer ABN Amro is surprising, ING says in a research note. Bloomberg reported that the Belgian lender was in the early stages of evaluating a potential deal earlier this week, sending ABN Amro's shares up around 10%. "We strongly believe that it is a very unlikely outcome," analyst Jason Kalamboussis writes. The bank has for years pursued a strategy to enter Central and Eastern European countries and also has state-owned insurer Ethias in its sights. There doesn't seem to be a real basis for an approach on ABN Amro, he adds, noting that KBC doesn't have experience in buying large assets. Such a move would also dilute its returns, while the compaby doesn't need to resort to a defensive move to protect is Benelux ambitions, Kalamboussis notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1023 GMT - The increase of Italian tycoon Francesco Gaetano Caltagirone's stake in Italian bank Monte Dei Paschi reduces risk for the stock, Equita says in a research note. Caltagirone's holding company asked for ECB authorization to increase its stake in the lender above 10%, to 10.9% following Monte Dei Paschi's takeover of Mediobanca--in which the holding company also owned a stake. Caltagirone is now Monte dei Paschi's second largest shareholder, after Delfin. The move leaves the possiblity of a further stake increase open, analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0934 GMT - Asian banks are likely to remain attractive thanks to domestic demand, local-currency earnings and above-average dividend yields, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. While Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts could pressure net-interest margins for some banks, Asian rates may not fall as much as U.S. rates, they note. These companies' return on equity is likely sustainable, MS analysts say, adding that Indian and Japanese banks are likely to see gains, which could support capital returns. Morgan Stanley maintains its overweight rating on Asia financials. "Key to the call on Asian banks is their ability to maintain earnings alongside their willingness and ability to maintain capital repayments to shareholders," they say. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0911 GMT - Nordea is one of the highest quality banks in Europe on a return-on-tangible-equity basis and offers superior returns, lower earnings volatility, and better free capital generation than many peers, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. The coming investor day is an opportunity for the Helsinki-based bank to demonstrate its Nordic scale advantage over peers, with a focus on growth opportunities and cost efficiency, he adds. "With a distribution yield of circa 10% per annum and offering high and resilient returns, the shares remain our only buy in the Nordic banks sector." Shares rise 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0618 GMT - TotalEnergies' buyback cut signals tougher market conditions that should drive it to reduce near-term investment, RBC analysts write. The French energy group late Wednesday said it planned a fourth-quarter share buyback to $1.5 billion, down from its recent quarterly rate of $2 billion. The company is likely to announce a reduction in organic capital expenditure for 2026 to $16 billion-$17 billion from about $18 billion previously presented at its Sept. 29 annual strategy update, RBC says. The analysts expect TotalEnergies to announce an option of further trimming spending as a protective strategy during future downturns. Shares rose 2.1% Wednesday to close at 53.50 euros and little changed year-to-date. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0246 GMT - Pertamina Geothermal Energy is likely a prime proxy for bets on geothermal energy, Bahana Sekuritas' Jeremy Mikael says in a research report. The Indonesian renewable energy company offers baseload electricity generation, making it more reliable compared to other renewable sources, the analyst says. Also, the company's valuation is more reasonable than that of other listed geothermal peers in Indonesia, reinforcing a scarcity value. The Indonesian company is on track to add 1.1GW over next decade with planned capacity additions totaling 280MW for 2027-2028, while its total capital expenditure for 2025-2034 is expected to reach $4.3 billon. The brokerage initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of IDR1,700.00. Shares are 0.7% lower at IDR1,370.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0118 GMT - Infratil's CDC data-centers business just took a big step toward derisking a target of doubling Ebitda across FY 2025-2027, Forsyth Barr says. CDC has signed an additional 100MW of contracts. This means it now has 95% of FY 2027 revenue contracted. "We increase our FY 2027 Ebitda estimate 7% due to this higher contracting, and are now in line with its A$660 million target," analyst Ben Crozier says. The contracts were signed just in time, according to Forsyth Barr. To achieve the Ebitda target, CDC needs most of its FY 2027 contracts to start generating revenue early in the year. Infratil's FY 2027 period starts in six months, and Forsyth Barr says the time from contract signing to revenue generating appears to sit at around 3-6 months. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2259 GMT [Dow Jones]--Amplitude Energy's A$150 million capital raising looks sensible to Bell Potter. That's because Amplitude's capex commitments will rise after management expanded a drilling program to support its East Coast Supply Project and signaled the potential restart of the Patricia Baleen field in southeastern Australia. Bell Potter says drilling a fourth well, specifically to target the Nestor deposit, could take ECSP capex to A$485 million-A$580 million. "We see the Nestor option further de-risking the ECSP through a portfolio effect," analyst Stuart Howe says. Bell Potter retains a buy call on Amplitude, and trims its price target by 3.4% to A$0.28/Share. Amplitude ended Wednesday at A$0.235. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1900 GMT - Crude futures post back-to-back gains amid rising tensions over Russia, with continuing Ukrainian attacks on Russian oil infrastructure raising concerns about supply. "The Russian bid continues to support crude oil prices," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. On the fundamental side, prices got additional support from the EIA's report of a 607,000 barrel decline in U.S. crude stocks, adding to the previous week's big drawdown even as imports rose and exports were lower. U.S. gasoline and diesel stocks also fell. WTI and Brent settle up 2.5% at$64.99 and $69.31 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1745 GMT - Energy companies will likely remain focused on returning capital to investors next year even as they seek to expand their assets, according to Wood Mackenzie. "Oil-and-gas companies are caught between competing pressures as they plan for 2026. Near-term price downside risks clash with the need to extend hydrocarbon portfolios," Tom Ellacott, senior vice president for corporate research at the energy-focused consulting firm, says in a report. "Meanwhile, shareholder return of capital and balance sheet discipline will constrain reinvestment rates." Ventures aimed at reducing carbon emissions are one area in which energy companies look to trim spending, Wood Mackenzie says. "Most large international oil companies and national oil companies will converge on allocating 10-20% of overall budgets to low-carbon initiatives," it says. "Capital allocation will swing back towards upstream investments." (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1348 GMT - Oil futures are higher on continued unease about Russian supplies, while the market looks to U.S. inventory data from the EIA. The API yesterday reported a 3.8 million decline in crude stocks, analysts note. Analysts in a WSJ survey predict a 300,000 barrel drop in crude inventories following the previous week's unexpectedly large 9.3 million barrel draw. EIA data are scheduled for release at 10:30 a.m. ET. WTI is up 1.5% at $64.37 a barrel, and Brent rises 1.4% to $68.59. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0846 GMT - Seatrium's planned sale of its U.S. shipbuilding facility in Texas is likely a good move, given the labor shortage and cost challenges that it's faced in recent years, say its bulls at CGS International. The S$65 million sale is likely to result in gains of S$26 million, progressively recognized, say analysts Lim Siew Khee and Meghana Kande in a note. The offshore and marine company is planning to deliver two legacy projects by end-2025, which could allow it to trim provisions for onerous contracts and reduce operating expenses by S$20 million next year. CGS International maintains an add rating and S$2.80 target price, noting that Seatrium is on track for core-profit turnaround. Shares are 0.8% lower at S$2.41. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0756 GMT - Shares in London-listed miners rose in early trading after copper prices surged overnight on Freeport McMoRan's declaration of force majeure at a crucial mine. Copper futures on the London Metal Exchange climbed to their highest levels in over a year after Freeport declared force majeure on supplies contracted from its Grasberg mine in Indonesia. The U.S. mining group's statement renewed supply concerns as the Grasberg site constitutes 3.2% of global copper supply and 70% of Freeport's copper production. Rio Tinto and Glencore shares rose 2.1% and 0.9% respectively, while Anglo American shares were up 1%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0458 GMT - Citi sees bigger-than-expected earnings for Australian gold miners ahead, after raising its own gold-price forecasts. The bank upgrades its FY 2026 earnings estimates for companies including Northern Star and Evolution Mining by 20% to 30%. As a result, it also raises its target prices for Australian gold stocks. Northern Star's target is raised to A$23.00 from A$18.00. Evolution's goes to A$10.50 from A$8.00. Those stocks trade at A$22.78 and A$10.38, respectively. Citi's top gold pick in the ASX100 is Evolution. It cites "organic growth options, asset quality and life, recent operational delivery coupled with the provision of a medium-term capex outlook/framework plus copper." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0248 GMT - Palm oil rises in early Asian trade. Sentiment is seen to be supported by Malaysia's strong export performance recently. Shipments for Sept. 1 to 20 rose between 8.3% and 8.7% on month, according to two key cargo surveyors, says Phillip Nova commodities strategist Darren Lim in a note. This is an improvement from mid-month estimates and a sign that external demand remains firm ahead of the festive period as buyers stock up, Lim says. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives December contract is 60 ringgit higher at 4,439 ringgit a ton.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0213 GMT - Iron ore falls in early Asian trade despite seeing a recent rebound alongside steel prices. Fundamentals suggest a weak outlook, unless China announces new infrastructure or property support measures, ANZ Research analysts write in a report. Leading property market indicators also remain downbeat, signaling that a significant improvement in steel consumption is unlikely, they say. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.4% lower at CNY800.5 a ton. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0135 GMT - For Mineral Resources, better debt terms are being offset by a softer lithium-price outlook, at least in the short run, say UBS analysts. In a note, the analysts say the miner's debt refinancing "signals increasing confidence." They reckon Mineral Resources can refinance its remaining bonds at roughly 7%, too. Yet UBS pares its near-term earnings per share estimates after trimming its spodumene price forecasts for this year and next. "Short term [lithium] downgrades impact near-term EPS but this is more than offset by cheaper debt in the long-term," they say. UBS keeps a neutral rating and A$43.20 target on the stock. Mineral Resources is down 1.0% at A$40.15. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2300 GMT - The sale of Deterra Royalties' gold offtake contracts returns its focus to industrial metals, differentiating the company from gold-royalty peers, Macquarie analysts say in a note. "Those looking to have exposure to the industrial metals complex for lower beta may find DRR an attractive alternative to higher operationally leveraged exposures," say the analysts. They reiterate a "neutral" rating and A$4.20 target. Deterra last traded at A$4.19. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1657 GMT - Lithium has quickly become a strategic resource essential to U.S. national security, clean energy, and economic competitiveness. The metal is used in a wide range of products from EVs, cell phones, and laptops to energy storage and medicine. While countries like Australia and Chile produce plenty of raw lithium, China dominates the processing, creating a supply chain vulnerability. For this reason, the U.S. is ramping up its involvement in lithium, from funding and regulation to potential ownership, because it sees the mineral as critical to its clean energy future and geopolitical independence. Jefferies analysts see a potential positive ripple effect if the U.S. government buys a stake in Lithium Americas, because investors are likely to see equity stakes as an indictor of a favorable return on invested capital. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1642 GMT - Lithium Americas may be getting another partner for its Thacker Pass project in Nevada. The mining company received a $2.26 billion loan under the special U.S. Department of Energy program to help build out the processing facilities at Thacker Pass, which is among the largest known lithium deposits in the U.S., 38% owned by General Motors. Construction began in March 2023 and has a total measured and indicated reserve of 44.5 million tons of lithium carbonate equivalent. A Reuters report suggested the U.S. government may consider up to a 10% stake in the Canadian miner, signaling the importance of the critical mineral to the U.S. economy and national security, considering China's dominance in processing the metal. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1618 GMT - A possible U.S. government stake in Lithium Americas would give its Thacker Pass project in Nevada a vote of confidence. The project, for which it is negotiating a $2.26 billion government loan, has come under scrutiny as Department of Energy officials questioned its viability and administration officials raised concerns about the company's ability to repay the loan given low lithium prices. TD Cowen analyst David Deckelbaum says in a report that "a government stake would lend credence to project completion and expansion to multiple phases with perhaps enhanced economics." Lithium Americas shares double in high volume trading. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1515 GMT - A potential U.S. government stake in Lithium Americas would a clear indication that the critical material is key to the future. Dan Ives of Wedbush Securities tells the WSJ it would be "a shot across the bow" and mean "the U.S. government is planting a flag on the lithium supply front." Lithium Americas nearly doubles following a Reuters report that the U.S. government is negotiating to take up to a 10% stake in the Canadian miner. Ives says it would be "an aggressive and smart move in our view in this arms race for energy." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1504 GMT - Lithium Americas is skyrocketing after the company says it's in talks with the U.S. Department of Energy and General Motors over terms of its $2.26 billion government loan for its large lithium project in Nevada. The news comes after Reuters reported the U.S. is considering taking up to a 10% equity stake in the company. Analysts at Jefferies say to the extent the US government believes equity stakes have a significantly lower political cost than tax increases, "mark-to-market equity stakes can offset deficit financing, higher deficit spending can support corporate profits, interest rates can trend lower to support equity valuations, and equity markets can view equity stakes as a leading indicator of favorable ROIC [return on invested capital]." Lithium Americas surges 98% to C$8.35.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0809 GMT - The German automotive industry continues to face significant challenges, despite the U.S. retroactively reducing tariffs on imports of passenger cars and car parts from the EU, the president of the German association of the automotive industry, Hildegard Mueller, says. Although the reduction is an important step, the burden imposed by additional tariffs on a range of steel and aluminum products remain, she says in a comment. The EU must continue to work to improve the framework conditions for transatlantic trade, Mueller says. The agreed cooperation on regulatory matters is an opportunity for both sides of the Atlantic and should form the basis for a positive agenda, she adds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - European defense stocks fall across the board in early morning trade Thursday, after surging on the back of the comments made by President Trump on the Ukraine war on Tuesday evening. Babcock International Group loses 2.5%, Chemring falls 1.8%, Hensoldt is down 2.2%, Rheinmetall drops 1.4% and Leonardo decreases 1.3%. Safran and Thales fall 0.7% and recover from deeper drops in opening trade. On Wednesday, European defense stocks led the Stoxx Europe 600 list of risers, after Trump said Kyiv could win back "all of Ukraine" and that Russia looks like a "paper tiger." (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0610 GMT - The Philippines' aviation sector is likely the central pillar to connect some growth drivers, such as airline expansion and a recovering tourism industry, Maybank Securities' Ronalyn Joyce Lalimo says in a research report. Capital is being invested to expand the Ninoy Aquino International Airport, the New Manila International Airport and regional hubs, as well as 10 public-private partnership projects worth PHP181 billion, the analyst notes. Supporting measures include the country's PHP7.5-billion Aviation Transport Infrastructure Program and 15-bundled airport upgrades worth PHP8 billion. The brokerage's top sector pick is Cebu Air, which has a buy rating and a target price of PHP48.00. Shares are 0.6% lower at PHP32.55. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0335 GMT - SATS is unlikely to be largely affected by softer U.S. air-cargo demand after the removal of the de minimis exception, says CGS International analysts in a note. Without the exception, which allowed parcels below US$800 to enter the country tax-free, U.S. air-cargo demand may weaken. Still, the analysts say any drag on global demand will likely be limited as e-commerce companies pivot to other markets. They expect SATS' cargo-business growth to outpace the industry with its cross-regional operations. They retain their conservative 2026-2028 EPS estimates given the air-cargo handler's sensitivity to macroeconomic conditions, but still expect an attractive 15.4% net profit CAGR over the same period. CGS International reiterates an add rating and a S$3.83 target on SATS, which falls 0.9% to S$3.42. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0306 GMT - NIO's stock has been volatile following the release of a key sports utility vehicle over the weekend. The automaker's shares listed in Hong Kong gained leading up to the launch but dropped sharply earlier this week before recovering some of the losses. The fall was likely driven by the view that NIO has no more near-term catalysts after the launch, says Eugene Hsiao, head of China equity strategy at Macquarie Capital. Product launches by other Chinese EV makers such as XPeng and Li Auto led to similar stock movements in the past, he says. Shares are up 2.1% at HK$56.60. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

1558 GMT - General Motors' North America segment looks likely to drive margins back to a target range of 8% to 10% as early as next year, even as added tariff costs remain a hurdle, UBS analysts say in a note. GM should benefit from lower tariff rates on imports from South Korea and potentially Mexico, as well as lower U.S. emissions standards, which would mean the company won't have to expense regulatory credits and can shift its portfolio to higher-profit vehicles, the analysts say. GM should also get a lift from its continued cost-cutting efforts and potential price hikes, helped by further U.S. rate cuts, they say. They upgrade the stock to buy from neutral and raise their price target to $81 from $56. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1328 GMT - AutoZone finished slightly weaker Tuesday after an $80 million tariff-driven accounting charge caused it to miss F4Q earnings and margin expectations. But the company's strong overall fundamentals show investor concerns are overblown, say Oppenheimer analysts. "We look upon underlying trends at AZO as stronger than headline figures suggest and view yesterday's modest pullback in shares as inconsistent with seemingly clear signals of improving fundamental momentum," the analysts say. The company recorded strong F4Q comparable sales growth and management expects at least 3% ticket inflation for the remainder of the year, the analysts say, adding that AutoZone remains confident in its ability to manage inflation dynamics with pricing and other mitigation efforts. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1242 GMT - AutoZone's F4Q results showed it is continuing to perform significantly better than other auto part retailers, say D.A. Davidson analysts Michael Baker and Keegan Cox. The gap between AutoZone's domestic comparable sales growth and the industry's was 350 basis points during AutoZone's F4Q. "That gap widened from 100 bps last quarter and was the widest since 2Q21 as AZO continues to take share at an accelerating pace and out-comp competitors," the analysts say. The company's performance is being fueled by investments in its distribution infrastructure, which is allowing it to take share in the commercial business. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1036 GMT - Rheinmetall is set for a near-term revenue boost as the German government moves to convert existing framework contracts into firm orders, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The German arms maker already sits on some of the largest framework agreements with the German defense ministry. If these become real contracts, then optional backlog transforms into near-term revenue, he notes. Russian probes into the North Atlantic Treaty Organization's airspace have increased pressure to get weapons delivered fast, Rieck adds. Governments will prioritize suppliers who can deliver at scale and on time, he says. Rheinmetall has already scaled assembly lines, staffed up and secured call offs, while many competitors have lagged behind in building new capacity, Rieck says. Mwb-research confirms its price target for the stock at 2,500 euros. Shares are up 1.3% at 1,936.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Mitchells & Butlers More Resilient Than Most U.K. Hospitality Businesses — Market Talk
data.description: Mitchells & Butlers is a more resilient company than most U.K. hospitality and medium-sized companies when it comes to attracting British consumers, Goodbody analyst Fintan Ryan says. The London-listed pub and restaurant operator has a strong track-record in managing inflationary cost pressures, and benefits from a diversified portfolio across regions and price points, Ryan says. Investors have showed caution toward hospitality stocks in recent times, which have posted more muted summer results, but Mitchells & Butlers continued to outperform, he notes. Shares are down 6.6% at 2.46 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Wanhua Chemical’s maleic anhydride plant sees improved productivity with Clariant’s catalyst
data.description:
Clariant AG has reported operational results for its SynDane 3142 LA catalyst at Wanhua Chemical's maleic anhydride plant in Yantai, China, as per Chemweek.

The company said since its startup in 2024, the catalyst has improved efficiency and sustainability in one of the world's largest maleic anhydride production facilities, which boasts a capacity of 200,000 metric tons per year.The SynDane catalyst has achieved notable performance metrics, including high yields and significantly reduced byproduct formation, demonstrating its effectiveness in optimizing the production process. Its design contributes to sustainability by minimizing pressure drop and lowering energy consumption, aligning with industry trends toward more eco-friendly manufacturing practices, it added.Clariant’s Applied Catalyst Technology team, based at Panjin, China, has been instrumental in providing comprehensive technical support throughout the project, ensuring the successful implementation and operation of the catalyst.According to the company, the SynDane 3142 LA is a selective oxidation catalyst featuring Clariant's proprietary double-alpha shape, which enhances its physical and chemical properties to maximize selectivity for maleic anhydride production. This unique design offers a distinct advantage over conventional ring-shaped catalysts by achieving higher productivity at reduced process and energy costs.In addition to its operational efficiency, SynDane ensures high-quality maleic anhydride by minimizing the formation of undesirable byproducts, which significantly reduces plant downtime, said Clariant. Due to its operating stability, the catalyst boasts a long life cycle of over five years, making it a reliable choice for producers looking to enhance their manufacturing processes.
